hand.title: Daily Hand

events.text: Your cards — 8, 7. You raised aggressively before the flop, putting pressure on your opponent, who calmly called to see the next cards.
Flop — 3, 4, 10. You bet to test the waters, and your opponent called, seemingly unimpressed by your aggression.
Turn — 10. You checked, giving your opponent the opportunity to take control, and they bet — bigger than expected, indicating a potential hand improvement.
River — 2. Your opponent fired again for value, trying to maximize their winnings with a confident bet.

wrongReasons: 6 3 has similar strength, but look at when the aggression shifted — the turn card connects with the correct hand in a way it doesn't connect with 6 3.
10 Q makes a three of a kind here — a hand that strong would have raised earlier and pushed harder for maximum value. The measured sizing in this hand points to something weaker.
A K doesn't benefit from the turn card the way the correct hand does. A player holding only one pair wouldn't have the confidence to raise on that street.
hand.explanation:
Answer: J 3

Your opponent's hand fits the betting pattern as they made one pair on the flop and then two pair on the turn with the J and 10, which led to their sudden aggression. The lack of improvement on the river didn't change their confident betting for value, confirming J 3 as their likely hand.
